title: Rust
edition_pinned: PC Steam, Meta Shift update (2 October 2025) — pins monument-gated workbenches while retaining upkeep, raiding, and server wipes.
moat: Loot progression leaves the base at monuments, while upkeep and raids make stored gains contestable.

loop_skeleton.description: Harvest enough to leave spawn, loot and recycle at monuments, unlock equipment, store it behind a maintained base, then contest another base or route.

depth_mechanisms.name: monument-gated workbenches
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Workbench tiers require fragments found through monument puzzles and events, pushing crafting progression into routes other players can contest.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If workbench gates can be completed inside the base, pre-tier-three player encounters at gated monuments decrease against matched servers using monument fragments.

depth_mechanisms.name: base upkeep curve
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: A tool cupboard consumes building resources, and the upkeep share rises with block count, so storage capacity and raid resistance carry recurring cost.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If added building blocks carry no recurring resource cost, the share of gathering time spent replacing tool-cupboard upkeep falls to zero and median base block count rises.

depth_mechanisms.name: loot-to-research conversion
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: A found item can be consumed with scrap at a research bench to unlock its blueprint, turning one contested drop into a future crafting option.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If found equipment cannot change later crafting access, recovering one item affects only the current fight and not the wipe's progression path.

depth_mechanisms.name: wipe-bounded accumulation
depth_mechanisms.mechanism: Server wipes remove map state on a schedule, converting base location, route knowledge, and progression speed into repeat-session decisions.
depth_mechanisms.falsifiable_claim: If servers never reset and decay cannot clear abandoned structures, access to unclaimed build sites decreases with server age instead of returning after the monthly reset.

feel_signature: Footsteps or gunfire interrupt harvesting; the player chooses whether to expose carried loot, hide it in a maintained base, or spend it on a raid.
second_session_answer: A return session inherits blueprint access and route knowledge but faces changed ownership, depleted bases, and a wipe clock that reprices every stockpile.
packaging_lessons: Ship harvesting, one monument route, recycling, one research tier, building upkeep, death-loot transfer, and save-state as one loop.
Place progression inputs at shared map destinations so equipment growth creates encounters instead of menu time.
Charge storage and defense after construction so the base remains a resource decision each session.
anti_lessons: Do not copy full-loot loss without recoverable routes, research carryover, and storage choices; loss alone only deletes time.
Do not copy wipe schedules into a solo loop; resets matter because players race and contest the same resource graph.

system_bom.note: harvest, monument run, research, build, raid, and defend must exchange resources
monuments, barrels, crates, and defeated players supply components and equipment
recipes and workbench tiers convert gathered resources into tools, weapons, and building parts
blueprints, scrap, fragments, research, and workbenches gate the wipe's equipment path
carried loot is exposed to death transfer until deposited or spent
death transfers carried items and raiding can transfer stored resources
fragments and workbenches can be exchanged between players
shared monuments, build sites, and bases make routes and ownership contestable
decay state survives server restarts, and scheduled wipes remove the procedural map state

citations.claim: The edition pin, moat, monument-gate depth entry, and progression BOM row rely on the October 2025 fragment requirements for workbenches and their monument and event sources.
The base-upkeep depth entry and third packaging lesson rely on tool-cupboard resource consumption, block-count tax bands, outside-in decay, and repair through paid upkeep.
The loot-to-research depth entry relies on found items, scrap, a research bench, and blueprint creation.
The wipe-bounded depth entry relies on the documented first-update-of-month wipe policy and the use of wipes for map and data changes.
The feel signature's interruption and exposure decision relies on progression resources being placed at monuments to generate clashes over territory.
The feel signature's store-or-spend decision relies on upkeep making stored resources carry a cost after construction.
The second-session answer's blueprint carryover relies on blueprint progression providing a reason to remain after death.
The second-session answer's changed ownership and depletion rely on decay state persisting across restarts and abandoned structures being removed.
The second-session answer's wipe clock relies on the documented monthly wipe policy.
The first packaging lesson's monument, recycling, and workbench chain follows the fragment and scrap path documented for the pinned update.
The first packaging lesson's research step follows the documented found-item, scrap, research-bench, and blueprint path.
The first packaging lesson's building-upkeep step follows the documented tool-cupboard resource charge and decay behavior.
The shared-destination packaging lesson relies on fragments being placed in monument puzzles and events to produce player encounters.
The loot, economy, and multiplayer BOM rows rely on monument loot, fragment conversion, fragment and workbench trading, and contested destinations.
The crafting and progression BOM rows rely on default blueprints, workbench tiers, found equipment, scrap research, and blueprint unlocks.
The inventory and win-loss-death BOM rows rely on corpses and destroyed storage dropping retrievable item containers whose lifetime follows their contents.
The save-state BOM row relies on decay state surviving server restarts and unattended structures continuing to decay.
The save-state BOM row's scheduled reset relies on the documented monthly procedural-map wipe policy.
The raid portions of the loop and win-loss-death BOM row rely on building blocks being attackable with tool-specific wear and explosive charges.
Anti-lesson — full-loot premise: corpses and destroyed storage leave retrievable item containers after loss.
Anti-lesson — full-loot recovery: blueprint research gives a found item an effect on later crafting access after the item is lost.
Anti-lesson — full-loot routes: monument gates place progression recovery on routes that other players contest.
Anti-lesson — full-loot storage: tool-cupboard upkeep makes the location and scale of stored protection a recurring resource choice.
Anti-lesson — wipe inference: Facepunch documents wipes as shared-server resets, so the repeat-session race depends on players contesting the same map.
